one_line_takeaway: Giving every GenAI user a distinct, maximally-dissimilar multi-bit watermark turns AI-content detection into forensic user attribution with provable, near-perfect accuracy at scale, but only inherits, and cannot exceed, the robustness of the underlying watermark, so it collapses under white-box removal attacks while resisting limited-query black-box ones.
key_finding: Without post-processing, detection and attribution are near-perfect even at very large user counts: FDR is nearly 0 for all three generators, the WORST-case per-user TAR_i/TDR_i exceeds 0.94, fewer than 0.1% of the 100,000 users have TAR_i/TDR_i below 0.98, and 85% of users reach a perfect 1.0 for Midjourney and DALL-E 2 (60% for Stable Diffusion). TDR and TAR curves overlap because the default tau=0.9 exceeds (1+alpha_i)/2 = 0.89, empirically confirming 'detection implies attribution.' The system inherits the underlying watermark's robustness: it stays accurate under common post-processing until image quality itself collapses (JPEG TDR/TAR drop sharply only when quality factor Q < 40, where SSIM also drops), is NOT robust to white-box adversarial post-processing (TDR/TAR driven to 0 with quality preserved), but IS effectively robust to black-box adversarial post-processing (WEvade-B-Q forces evasion but only by badly degrading image SSIM even with many queries). A-BSTA selection beats NRG which beats Random on worst-case TAR (it produces smaller alpha_i). Theory matches empirics closely for TDR/TAR (tight lower bounds), while the FDR upper bound is loose (conservative because it treats all bits as equiprobable). Aggregating multiple contents from the same user via majority vote raises average TAR from 0.998 to 1.000.
core_contribution: The first systematic study of watermark-based, USER-LEVEL attribution of AI-generated content (as opposed to the user-agnostic AI-vs-not detection that companies already deploy). The paper's own contributions are: (1) a framework that assigns each registered user of a GenAI service a unique multi-bit watermark, embeds that user's watermark into every content the user generates, and attributes a given content to the user whose stored watermark has the highest bitwise accuracy with the watermark decoded from the content (detection = the best match exceeds a threshold tau); (2) a rigorous probabilistic (binomial) analysis giving a lower bound on the per-user True Detection Rate (TDR_i, Thm 1), a lower bound on the per-user True Attribution Rate (TAR_i, Thm 4), and two upper bounds on the False Detection Rate (FDR, Thms 2 and 3), parameterized by a per-user accuracy beta_i and a global randomness gamma of the watermarking method; (3) the observation that these bounds improve as users' watermarks become more DISSIMILAR, leading to a formal 'watermark selection' optimization (minimize the maximum pairwise bitwise accuracy among users), a proof that it is NP-hard via reduction from the farthest-string problem, and A-BSTA, an efficient approximate online selection algorithm that assigns each new registrant a maximally-dissimilar watermark. A key theoretical/empirical result is 'detection implies attribution': when tau > (1+alpha_i)/2, TDR_i and TAR_i nearly coincide, so once content is correctly detected it is also correctly attributed.
threat_model: Malicious users of a GenAI cloud service generate harmful content (political deepfakes, disinformation/propaganda, non-consensual imagery, false copyright claims) and distribute it anonymously. The defender is the GenAI service provider (or law enforcement) who controls registration, the encoder, the watermark database, and the detection API, and wants forensic traceability from a piece of content back to the originating user so it can suspend/ban offenders or support investigations. Adversaries also actively try to defeat the system: (a) evasion via post-processing an already-watermarked image to remove/corrupt the watermark, in a white-box setting (attacker has the decoder) or a black-box setting (attacker can only query the detection API a limited number of times, e.g. via WEvade-B-Q); and (b) watermark FORGERY, where an attacker crafts content to be falsely attributed to a targeted innocent user (framing). The paper explicitly scopes OUT how the candidate user set U is constructed, and treats developing new robust watermarking primitives as orthogonal (it reuses existing watermarks). Detection alone is assumed insufficient because it does not reveal who produced the content.
limitations: (1) Robustness is inherited, not created: against white-box adversarial post-processing the scheme fails outright (TAR/TDR to 0 with image quality intact); its 'good robustness' claim holds only in the limited-query black-box regime. (2) The FDR upper bound (Thm 3) is loose because the analysis assumes equiprobable bits, which real watermarks violate. (3) Empirical validation caps at s=100,000 (and one s=1,000,000 run) due to compute; the headline '100 million users' result is purely THEORETICAL and rests on assumed parameters (beta_i=0.99, alpha_i=0.2, gamma=0.05). (4) All experiments are on images resized to 128x128; text/audio/video are only argued conceptually (text watermarking limited to bitstring-based methods, excluding e.g. Kirchenbauer-style green-list watermarks). (5) The candidate-user set construction is out of scope, yet real-world attribution accuracy depends on it (a truly open-world attacker not in U cannot be attributed). (6) HiDDeN degrades on longer watermarks (n up to 80), constraining capacity/scalability. (7) Watermark forgery (framing) is only discussed, leaning on cited steganalysis results (Table 6), not a core defended-against threat. (8) Privacy of a per-user tracing database is acknowledged but only sketched (trusted-verifier / PKI-style design).

datasets_used: AI-generated images from Stable Diffusion (public dataset, Wang et al. 2023) - 10,000 train / 1,000 test
AI-generated images from Midjourney (Turc & Nemade 2022) - 10,000 train / 1,000 test
AI-generated images from DALL-E 2 (Images 2023) - 10,000 train / 1,000 test
Non-AI-generated set: combination of COCO, ImageNet, and Conceptual Caption, 1,000 images sampled uniformly at random
quantitative_results: FDR nearly 0 for all three generators when no post-processing is applied
Fewer than 0.1% of the 100,000 users have TAR_i/TDR_i < 0.98
85% of users achieve TAR_i/TDR_i = 1.0 for Midjourney and DALL-E 2; 60% for Stable Diffusion
Default parameters: s=100,000 users, watermark length n=64, threshold tau=0.9; tau (0.9) > (1+alpha_i)/2 = 0.89, so TDR and TAR curves overlap
Common post-processing: average TDR/TAR fall sharply only when JPEG quality factor Q < 40 (SSIM also collapses there)
White-box adversarial post-processing drives TDR/TAR to 0 while preserving image quality; black-box WEvade-B-Q forces evasion (TDR/TAR = 0) but with severely degraded SSIM even at large query budgets
Majority-vote aggregation over multiple contents from one user raises average TAR from 0.998 to 1.000 (HiDDeN, Stable Diffusion, s=100,000, n=64, tau=0.9)
Theoretical extrapolation to 100 million users keeps TDR and TAR very close to 1 (assuming beta_i=0.99, alpha_i=0.2, gamma=0.05)
BSTA is not scalable: >8 hours to select even 16 watermarks (motivating A-BSTA, recursion depth capped at 8)
A-BSTA > NRG > Random on worst-1,000-user ranked TAR under JPEG Q=90 (A-BSTA yields smaller alpha_i)
baselines_compared: Random watermark selection (uniform random per user; equivalent to hashing user IDs)
NRG (Chen et al. 2016) - state-of-the-art approximate farthest-string algorithm, extended to watermark selection
BSTA (Gramm et al. 2003) - exact bounded search tree algorithm (not scalable, used as conceptual reference)
Watermarking primitives evaluated as the underlying method: HiDDeN (Zhu et al. 2018), StegaStamp (Tancik et al. 2020), PRC watermark (Gunn et al. 2025; Stable Diffusion only, needs inverse diffusion)
Adversarial attack baseline: WEvade-B-Q (Jiang et al. 2023b) black-box query attack
